meta.title: CLS 350d/400d Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Mercedes CLS 350d & 400d OM656 engines — real UK costs, Camtronic & fuel system insight, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

itemListElement.name: OM656 D29 R SCR (350d) / OM656 D29 SCR (400d)

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CLS 350d or 400d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM656 2.9-litre diesel engine typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £3,500–£5,500. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Fitting adds £1,300–£2,000 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines can exceed £14,000.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CLS 350d or 400d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM656 is generally considered a reliable engine — a modern, efficient straight-six diesel that replaced the OM642 V6. However, it has known issues: Camtronic variable valve timing problems (camshaft rocker wear causing popping intake noises), Bosch CP4 fuel system sensitivity (poor fuel damages piezo injectors), turbocharger issues (oil deposits, actuator hose failure), and AdBlue/DPF system faults. Regular maintenance and quality fuel are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes CLS 350d and 400d?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are Camtronic camshaft rocker wear (popping intake noise, rough idle, engine warning light), fuel system issues (Bosch CP4 injectors sensitive to fuel quality), turbocharger faults (oil deposits, actuator issues, boost lag), vacuum oil pump issues (early cars, 25–40k miles), and AdBlue/DPF system problems.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes CLS diesel engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £35,000 CLS 400d, a £7,000 recon is 20% — worth it. Against a tired £15,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% Mercedes threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes CLS diesel engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. CLS 350d: OM656 D29 R SCR. CLS 400d: OM656 D29 SCR. Both are 2.9-litre straight-six twin-turbo diesel engines. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 'CLS 350 d' or 'CLS 400 d'.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the camshaft rocker wear on the OM656, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Camshaft rocker wear is a known issue that will show up as a popping sound in the intake. Scoring of the cam lobes only happens when the car is driven with the inlet popping for thousands of miles. Repair costs typically range from £800–£2,500. If ignored, the issue can lead to cam lobe scoring, timing failure, and catastrophic engine damage. At the first sign of popping intake noises, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the single most effective preventative measure.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the OM656 2.9-litre straight-six diesel engine. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Used OM656 engines are available from approximately £3,500–£5,500.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a CLS OM656 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The straight-six layout and engine bay packaging make this a moderate-to-complex job.

problems.title: Camtronic Variable Valve Timing Issues — Camshaft Rocker Wear
problems.symptoms: Popping noise from the intake manifold, rough idle, engine warning light, loss of power, metallic ticking.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (camshaft rocker and actuator replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if left untreated leads to cam lobe scoring and timing failure)
problems.recommendation: Cam rocker wear is a known issue on the OM656. It will show up as a popping sound in the intake. Scoring of the cam lobes only happens when the owner has driven the car with the inlet popping away for a few thousand miles. If you notice unusual noises from the engine, have the Camtronic system inspected immediately. Regular oil changes are critical for this engine.

failureLink.label: Read the full Camtronic failure guide

problems.title: Fuel System Sensitivity — Bosch CP4 Injector Issues
problems.symptoms: Rough running, misfires, poor acceleration, engine warning light, increased fuel consumption.
problems.repairCost: £600–£2,000 (injector replacement — up to six piezo injectors)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if fuel system damage leads to engine failure)
problems.recommendation: The OM656 uses a Bosch CP4 common-rail system with sensitive piezo injectors. It does not tolerate poor-quality diesel or contamination. Even minor impurities in the fuel tank can damage the injectors, while a clogged fuel filter disrupts combustion and leads to power loss. Regular fuel filter changes and the use of high-quality diesel can significantly extend engine life. Bad fuel can also cause soot buildup on valves and intake channels.

failureLink.label: Read the full fuel injector failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger & AdBlue/DPF System Issues
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, unusual noises from the engine bay (whistling, grinding), turbo lag, DPF warning light, AdBlue system errors, engine management light.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (turbocharger repair or replacement; AdBlue/DPF system repairs)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if turbo failure or emissions system damage leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The OM656's BorgWarner R2S turbocharger is a sensitive component. Oil deposits, worn bearings, or a contaminated charge air system can lead to power loss and unusual noises. A known turbo actuator issue involves a hose that gets too hot and collapses, causing boost faults. The AdBlue and DPF systems are also sensitive to poor fuel quality and short journeys. Regular regeneration drives and high-quality AdBlue minimise risks.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This CLS Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with evidence of regular oil changes using MB 229.5 or 229.51 spec oil
* Camtronic system inspected or serviced (no popping intake noises)
* Fuel system regularly maintained (quality fuel, regular filter changes)
* Early OM656 vacuum oil pump issue resolved (if applicable)
avoidIf: * Popping noise from the intake (cam rocker wear warning)
* Loss of power or turbo lag (turbo or actuator issues)
* Poor service history — the OM656 is a sophisticated engine that demands good maintenance
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLS 350d and 400d are powered by Mercedes' modern OM656 straight-six diesel — a sophisticated and refined engine that delivers exceptional performance and efficiency. The 350d offers 286 hp and 600 Nm, while the 400d delivers 340 hp and 700 Nm. Both are generally reliable engines, but they have known weak points: Camtronic valve timing issues, Bosch CP4 fuel system sensitivity, turbocharger concerns, and AdBlue/DPF system faults. These are well-understood and manageable with proper maintenance. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained CLS diesel is a £25,000–£50,000 premium four-door coupe; a neglected one is a £7,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare CLS diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes CLS 350d or 400d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for popping noises from the intake (cam rocker wear warning)
* Service History — including the OM656 era (2017–present) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.5/229.51 spec oil
* Camtronic Check — Any history of camshaft rocker or actuator issues?
* Fuel System Check — Evidence of regular fuel filter changes and quality fuel use
* Turbo Check — Any loss of power, turbo lag, or unusual noises? (turbo or actuator warning)
* DPF/AdBlue Check — Any DPF or AdBlue warning lights or regeneration issues?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved Camtronic, fuel, turbo, or emissions system issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
